name: LiCoMemory
maintainer: Zhengjun Huang, Zhoujin Tian, Qintian Guo, Fangyuan Zhang, Yingli Zhou, Di Jiang, Xiaofang Zhou — affiliations: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology, Huawei Hong Kong Research Center, The Chinese University of Hong Kong Shenzhen, WeBank
license: [uncertain] Research code at https://github.com/EverM0re/LiCoMemory — license type not confirmed from available search results
last_major_release: [uncertain] arXiv preprint v1 published 2025-11-03; v2 available; no formal version tags observed
activity: [uncertain] GitHub repository at EverM0re/LiCoMemory; early-stage research release as of 2026-04; star count and contributor count not confirmed
storage_backend: [uncertain] CogniGraph is described as an in-memory or file-backed hierarchical graph structure; no explicit Neo4j, Postgres, or vector-DB backend is named in the paper. The graph stores session-summary nodes and entity-relation nodes linked by hyperlinks to source text chunks.
representation_primitive: entity+edge with episodic-note layering. CogniGraph is a two-layer hierarchical graph: (1) Session Level — nodes store textual summaries of user-assistant interaction sessions plus distilled keywords; (2) Entity-Relation Level — a lightweight knowledge graph of entities and relations extracted from dialogue content, where each entity node and relation edge retains only essential identifiers and is hyperlinked to its corresponding session summary. This decouples content storage (text chunks) from semantic organization (graph topology), making the graph a semantic index rather than a static document store.
api_shape: Python library. Code available at https://github.com/EverM0re/LiCoMemory. No first-party MCP server. No REST API or CLI described in the paper.
hosting: Self-host only. Research code release; no managed cloud or SaaS offering.
llm_dependency: Both ingest and retrieval. Ingest: LLM used to extract entities, relations, and keywords from dialogue chunks and generate session summaries. Retrieval: LLM used to parse the query into structured entities (anchors) that drive hierarchical top-down graph traversal. Evaluated primarily with Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct-Turbo and GPT-4o-mini as LLM backbones.
schema_extensibility: LLM-learns (open discovery). Entities and relations are extracted by the LLM during ingest; the graph schema is not prescribed — new entity types and relation types emerge dynamically from the dialogue content. Session-level keywords are also LLM-generated. This is an open-discovery approach analogous to Graphiti's entity extraction.
schema_richness: Dynamic / open-ended. Core structural layers: 2 (Session nodes, Entity nodes). Core edge types: CONTAINS (session-to-entity hyperlink), RELATION (entity-to-entity), TEMPORAL (ordering between sessions). Multi-label: not explicitly described. Multi-graph: single CogniGraph instance with two logical layers. Compared to Gimle §5 target: LiCoMemory's schema is intentionally minimalist — it does not distinguish file/symbol/module/API-endpoint/UI-component/decision/iteration entities. Its strength is the layered retrieval architecture, not schema breadth.
temporal_model: event-log / session-ordered. Each session node encodes a temporal position in the conversation timeline. The retrieval reranker applies temporal-awareness to prefer recent and temporally relevant sessions. However, there is no formal bi-temporal model (no valid_from/to + recorded_at separation); the temporal dimension is implicit in session ordering and used as a retrieval signal, not a queryable property.
forgetting_policy: none described. No LRU, semantic-dedup, or soft-delete mechanism in the paper. The graph grows monotonically with new sessions.
ingest_latency_class: real-time-incremental. Dialogue chunks are inserted session-by-session without triggering retrieval; the paper reports notably reduced update latency compared to baselines such as Mem0 and Zep. Chunk-by-chunk insertion emulates real-world conversational flows.
retrieval_modes: graph-traversal (hierarchical top-down) + semantic similarity (entity matching + reranking). The retrieval flow: (1) parse query into entity anchors; (2) match anchors against session-summary keywords (BM25-style overlap + semantic similarity) to rank sessions; (3) traverse entity-relation layer using anchors to locate relevant triples; (4) unified reranker jointly considers semantic similarity, hierarchical structure, and temporal relevance to produce final context.
retrieval_agenticity: multi-hop / structured top-down. Retrieval is non-trivially structured: query entities anchor a top-down traversal from abstract session summaries to specific entity triples. The integrated reranker with semantic + hierarchical + temporal signals constitutes a form of CRAG (contextual reranking). No explicit Self-RAG or CLAUSE-budget-aware policy.
retrieval_budget_control: no. No explicit per-query cost/latency budget parameters are described. Retrieval depth is implicitly bounded by the two-layer graph structure.
multi_project_namespace: none described. Designed for single-agent/single-user conversation memory. No multi-project partitioning or cross-project query support.
multi_user_access: none. Single-agent conversational memory system. No RBAC or provenance-aware sharing.
embedder_options: [uncertain] The paper uses semantic similarity for entity matching and reranking but does not specify the embedding model or provider. Likely OpenAI or sentence-transformers given the evaluation setup, but not confirmed.
llm_client_options: Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct-Turbo (via Together AI or similar) and GPT-4o-mini evaluated. LLM is required for both ingest (entity/relation extraction, summarization) and retrieval (query parsing). Cannot be disabled.
cost_privacy: [uncertain] Token cost per ingest involves at least two LLM calls per session: entity extraction + summarization. Token cost per query involves at least one LLM call for query entity parsing. The paper does not report absolute token counts. Compared to full-context baselines, LiCoMemory reduces token consumption substantially (the paper reports efficiency improvements over Mem0). Data leaves host if using remote LLM APIs (OpenAI, Together AI).
code_artifact_linkage: none. LiCoMemory is a conversational/dialogue memory system. It does not model code artifacts, file paths, symbols, or any code-structural entities. Not applicable to code repository contexts.
code_analysis_features: none. No call graph, diff-awareness, symbol index, or code-specific analysis. Purely dialogue/conversational domain.
memory_os_layer: plaintext (external memory). Operates at the external-storage tier — CogniGraph is a persistent graph structure outside model weights. No parametric or activation (KV-cache) tier. Cross-type migration not supported.
self_improvement_mechanism: none described. No LLM-reflection loop, no feedback from execution results. The graph is updated incrementally with new sessions but without self-evaluation or re-scoring of existing memories.
provenance_standard: none. No audit trail, W3C-PROV, or OpenTelemetry traces. Source text chunks are linked via hyperlinks from entity nodes to session summaries, providing implicit provenance at the session granularity.
benchmark_results: LongMemEval (GPT-4o-mini backbone): Accuracy=73.8%, Recall=76.6% — outperforms Mem0, A-Mem, Zep, and LoCoMo-RAG; best subset gains: Multi-Session (+26.6 pp Acc.), Temporal Reasoning (+15.9 pp Acc.) vs second-best baseline (Mem0). LoCoMo (Llama-3.1-70B): +7.5% Accuracy, +5.2% Recall vs Mem0g; (GPT-4o-mini): +8.9% Accuracy, +4.95% Recall. Latency: 10–40% reduction vs baselines. Ablation: removing structured retrieval drops full-dataset from 73.8/80.6 to 51.6/56.4, and multi-session from 64.7/73.5 to 40.6/48.2.
strengths: 1. Hierarchical top-down retrieval: abstract session summaries act as a coarse filter before fine-grained entity-triple lookup, dramatically reducing irrelevant context and cutting token consumption — directly actionable as a layering pattern for Gimle's Graphiti schema. 2. State-of-the-art on LongMemEval (73.8%) with low ingest latency: demonstrates that a lightweight graph index (no heavyweight vector DB) can outperform embedding-heavy systems like Mem0 and Zep on temporal and multi-session reasoning. 3. Real-time incremental updates: session-by-session insertion with reduced latency suits Gimle's requirement for live ingest of agent activities without batch rebuild.
weaknesses: 1. Dialogue-only domain: CogniGraph is purpose-built for conversational memory (user/assistant turns). It has no code-artifact, project, or repository modeling. Direct adoption for Gimle's codebase-understanding use case requires substantial schema extension. 2. No formal temporal model: temporal ordering is implicit in session sequence, not queryable as a bi-temporal property. Gimle §5's requirement for valid_from/to + recorded_at bi-temporal metadata is not met. 3. Minimal forgetting/lifecycle management: the graph grows unboundedly; no compaction, deduplication, or invalidation policy described.
fit_with_gimle_section_5: Verdict: steal-specific-ideas. The CogniGraph hierarchical layering pattern is directly actionable for Gimle's Graphiti-based schema. Specific ideas: (1) Two-tier graph indexing — Gimle should implement a Summary node layer (e.g., :Iteration or :Session summary) that hyperlinks to specific :Symbol/:File/:Decision nodes. This mirrors CogniGraph's session-summary-to-entity-triple structure. The top-down retrieval pattern (broad summary scan first, then specific node traversal) is superior to flat vector search for multi-hop project context. Example Cypher pattern derivable from LiCoMemory: MATCH (s:IterationSummary)-[:CONTAINS]->(e:Symbol) WHERE s.keywords CONTAINS $query_keyword RETURN e. (2) Unified reranker with temporal + hierarchical + semantic signals — Gimle's retrieval layer should combine semantic similarity (embedding cosine), graph-structural distance (hop count from anchor node), and temporal recency (recorded_at field). LiCoMemory's reranker architecture justifies building this as a first-class component rather than a post-hoc filter. (3) Keyword-indexed session summaries — storing distilled keywords on :Iteration/:Decision nodes enables fast coarse-grained lookup before deep graph traversal, reducing retrieval latency for common query patterns.
